We also experimented with sampling just 100 train instances for tuning the threshold.

subsections.title: Evaluation.
paragraphs: As common in classification tasks, we report the F1F_{1} measure.

subsections.title: Results
paragraphs: Table 9 presents the classification results on the three specific Type evaluations. Our zero-shot classifier, which only uses the training sets to find an optimal threshold for the corresponding Type, is able to identify tips and opinions effectively, and opinion with reason fairly well, on their respective benchmarks.888Chen et al. (2022) do not provide any intrinsic baseline results on opinion classification to which we can compare. In addition, we manipulated the original data from clause-level to sentence-level. Moreover, limiting the training sets to only 100 samples appears to hardly have any effect on the quality of the model, showing its robustness to paucity of labeled data.

For the tip task, we see in Table 10 that the predictor performs on par or much better than existing supervised baselines from Hirsch et al. (2021). Although our definition of tip differs slightly from that used in annotating the benchmark, our predictor is still reliable and useful. When the warning subtype of a tip is removed from the data (which, as mentioned before, generally do not fit our notion of a tip), the results dramatically improve.

We turn to exemplify how the combination of the varying Types in the typology serves as a strong \sayfeature set for addressing many downstream tasks that apply to review data. Here, we address the tasks of review helpfulness, sentence helpfulness, and sentiment analysis. We build models that use only the predicted typology labels as input (without further access to the text itself), with the goal not to outperform state of the art results, but rather to exhibit the strong signals concealed within the varying Types and their combinations.

subsections.title: Review Helpfulness
paragraphs: Predicting helpfulness of reviews is a popular task (Ocampo Diaz and Ng, 2018) due to its essential role in highlighting useful information to customers on e-commerce sites. In order to assess how different subsets of the Types assist with helpfulness prediction, we extract Amazon reviews (He and McAuley, 2016) and identify helpful and non-helpful reviews based on \sayup-votes and \saydown-votes on the reviews (∼\sim1000 balanced reviews, details in Appendix C.1). Each sentence from a review is input to our classifier (§3.1), producing a 24-dimensional vector of Type probabilities. The average of these vectors then represents the review-level Type probabilities.777Notice that each Type has an independent probability, and probabilities in a vector do not add up to 100%, since this is a multi-label classification setup. We reuse this averaging procedure throughout the rest of our experiments and analyses.

We then train a binary SVM classifier (Cortes and Vapnik, 1995) over the vectors to classify reviews as helpful or non-helpful, and evaluate its performance through cross-validation. We report classification accuracy, as is common in binary classification setups (Yadav and Vishwakarma, 2020). As seen in Table 2 (leftmost results column), using the full Types vector as an input produces a respectable accuracy of 72.6%, while applying only certain subsets of Types results in significantly (p<0.001p<0.001) lower scores, emphasizing the importance of the variability in the typology.

On further analysis, the results indicate that the opinion with reason Type is considerably more vital for helpfulness of a review than the opinion Type, possibly because providing reasoning for an opinion is more convincing. This is noticeable through the immense gap between the second and third rows of Table 2, and is further illustrated in Figure 2a, which shows probability scores for each Type aggregated at the review level and stratified based on review helpfulness. Generally, the subjective Types (which include opinions—refer to section 2) most strongly signal helpfulness, however the combination of all Types performs best. Results on all Type subsets are available in Table 6 in the appendix.

The bottom section of Table 2 presents the results when using the coarse-grained types (from section 2) as features for an SVM model, where the predicted fine-grained Types are mapped to their respective coarse-grained types. The fine-grained typology yields better results than the coarse-grained one, reiterating its benefit. The two random binary baselines are for lower-bound reference. The first baseline randomly chooses an answer at 50%, while the other chooses an answer assuming it knows the distribution of the gold labels.

subsections.title: Review Sentence Helpfulness
paragraphs: The next experiment assesses helpfulness of individual sentences in reviews (as opposed to full reviews), a task introduced by Gamzu et al. (2021) as a form of extreme product review summarization. Their review sentence dataset is annotated for helpfulness along a range from 0 (not helpful) to 2 (very helpful). We produce the Type vectors for the sentences and train a linear regression model to predict helpful sentence scores. In addition, we generate a binary classification task by separating the data into subsets of helpful and unhelpful sentences, taken from the top and bottom tertiles (see Appendix C.2), on which we train an SVM model.

We compare our results for the regression task with those of Gamzu et al. (2021) in Table 3. As in the review helpfulness task in §4.1, it is apparent that the Type-vectors provide a strong signal for predicting helpfulness also on the sentence level. The results of the binary classification task, shown in Table 2 (center results column), reinforce this conclusion. When comparing the aggregate Type probability vectors of helpful sentences to those of unhelpful sentences, Figure 2b exhibits large contrast on the opinion with reason, personal info and product description Types. The subjective Type subset provides the strongest signal, however the combination of all fine-grained Types substantially improves over all subsets, again emphasizing the importance of the variability in our typology.

subsections.title: Review Sentiment Polarity
paragraphs: Over the years, the customer review domain has been central in the task of sentiment analysis (Chen et al., 2017; Tesfagergish et al., 2022). Understanding the sentiment in reviews assists in surfacing positive and negative criticism on products or services. This in turn aids new customers in making more informed purchasing decisions, and concurrently facilitates improvement on the vendors’ side.

While, at first glance, the information Types in our typology should not associate with sentiment in any obvious manner, we perform a similar classification assessment as in the previous experiments. In this case, we randomly extract 5,000 Amazon reviews (He and McAuley, 2016) and group them into positive and negative reviews (review ratings of {4,5}\{4,5\}, or {1,2,3}\{1,2,3\} respectively, as commonly handled (Shivaprasad and Shetty, 2017)). Again training and evaluating an SVM through cross-validaition, we classify the reviews into their sentiment polarities (details in Appendix C.3).

Surprisingly, the SVM classifier is able to separate positive from negative reviews with an accuracy of 88.1%, as seen in Table 2 (rightmost column). Specifically, the combination of the subjective and stylistic Type-sets provides the signal, while all other Type-sets fail at identifying a distinction (the SVM classifier simply marks all sentences as positive). On closer inspection, Figure 2c shows that the improvement desire and stylistic Types indeed differ markedly between the positive and negative reviews, which offers a plausible explanation for the results.

While many models addressing the sentiment analysis task lack explainability for the sentimental decision (Adak et al., 2022), the information Types give a hint at what induces the sentimental polarity, and can further direct attention at specific features. In this case, by focusing on sentences that express an improvement desire, review readers can more efficiently understand what to expect from the product, and can refrain from reading full negative reviews that are rich in rhetorical information.

The three experiments above suggest that the type of content in reviews offers strong indicators for helpfulness and sentiment, and that the residual textual content past the types may be only of secondary importance. Moreover, utilizing fine-grained types provides stronger signals than coarse-grained ones, since, apparently, specific types of content distinctly influence humans’ perception of helpfulness and sentiment.

As illustrated in §4, our typology offers an instrument to analyze product-related texts and interpret differences between subsets of texts. In this section we focus on analyzing the structure of reviews and of review summaries. The analysis sheds light on the content patterns that reviewers choose to voice and what summarizers focus on when space is limited.

The AmaSum product reviews summarization dataset (Bražinskas et al., 2021) consists of about 31K products, each associated with a list of reviews and a reference summary taken from online platforms for professional product reviews. Each summary comprises verdict, pros and cons sections. We perform some filtering heuristics and then sample 100 products with their reviews and summaries, representing five different product categories (see App. C.4), for a total of 7,729 reviews and 100 summaries.

We compare Type prominence between reviews and summaries by aggregating their probability vectors (averaging review- or summary-level Type vectors), presented in Figure 3. Comparing the Type probabilities in reviews and summaries, it is clear (and unsurprising) that summaries are considerably more efficient in communicating information. In particular, summaries predominantly describe the product, provide reasoning for opinions and offer many tips. These findings are consistent with Bražinskas et al. (2021), who describe the verdict as emphasizing the most important points about the product, and the pros and cons as providing details about different aspects of the product.

Reviewers seem to add a non-negligible amount of personal information to the parts eventually excluded from summaries, possibly to make their reviews more relatable, for example, \sayWe had used this book when my children were young and were identifying flowers. Interestingly, according to our analysis of helpfulness (Figure 2b), readers seem to find this characteristic to be less helpful. In general, we find (Table 4) a much higher correlation between the vector of summaries and that of helpful sentences, than between the vector of summaries and that of unhelpful sentences. In other words, good summaries tend to contain mainly helpful sentences. On the other hand, a helpful review has a lower correlation with summaries, meaning that it still contains some potential verbosity, which is natural given the less stringent length constraints.

We conduct an examination of the reviews at the product category level to learn what kind of information reviewers tend to share for different kinds of products. Figure 4 reveals, for example, that people share much more personal information when reviewing toys and games. We find that reviewers purchase these mostly for others, especially children, and therefore provide an explanation of who they bought the product for and their experience with it, e.g., \sayHopefully my 3 year old doesn’t toss all of the pieces everywhere. A review is also, expectedly, more likely to discuss setup of an electronics device than for other kinds of products. A book review is more likely to describe a situation or provide a tip, two Types we find often co-occurring within sentences suggesting usage for the book, e.g., \sayThis book should probably be combined with other study methods to help your child perform to the best of their ability. We suggest that this behaviour may be specific to certain subcategories of books, such as self-help and other nonfiction. Overall, analyzing variability of Types across product categories allows data-driven discovery of insights about products and customers.

Rhetorical Structure Theory studies the organization of a body of text and the relationship between its parts (Mann and Thompson, 1988), facilitating the assessment of a text’s coherence. In our setting, we can examine how Types affect each other as a review or summary progresses. To demonstrate this, we compute the average Type probabilities at each sentence position of reviews with 6 sentences, and summaries with 7 sentences (explained in Appendix C.4), and plot them on a graph showing the progression.

As can be seen in Figure 5, reviews tend to open and close with a buy decision statement. Customers use such statements to introduce the situation, e.g., \sayPurchased this SSD in order to put Windows 7 and some games on it to decrease load times., or conclude with an emphasizing reiteration of satisfaction (or lack thereof), e.g., \sayI only regret not getting it sooner! Conversely, reasoned opinions and detailed information tend to occur in the middle of the review. We note that the likelihood of speculative statements increases as the review progresses. Speculation should be delivered only after it is justified, e.g., \sayThat being said, they’re probably still ok for temporary storage.

AmaSum summaries are formed by concatenating a verdict (avg. 1.42 sentences in our data), a pros section (avg. 4.04 sentences) and a cons section (avg. 1.54 sentences). Due to this segmented configuration, the rhetorical structure is only partly relevant since global coherence is not an objective of the summary. Figure 6 demonstrates the behavior of the verdict at the first 1–2 sentences, the next ∼\sim4 sentences representing the structure of the pros, and the last 1–2 corresponding to the cons section. The verdict embodies features that stand out most in the product with its overall sentiment, e.g., \saySingle camera setup that’s reasonably priced because it doesn’t require a separate base station. Indeed, the product description and opinion with reason Types are particularly likely. The pros section generally describes advantages of the product ordered by importance, and accordingly we see reasoning and tip content peaking and then decreasing gradually along these sentences. The cons section provides increased speculation to suggest what the disadvantage might cause, e.g., \sayThe 16-hour battery life can become frustrating for some, as well as vastly increased incidence of improvement desire, such as \sayCustomers commented that these aren’t absorbent enough to handle big messes.
Future work may further examine review and summary structure through the lens of our typology to improve automatic review summarization and to control content type while maintaining coherence.

Product reviews contain a wealth of information that has been mined only shallowly over the years. We introduce a broad typology of information types that we show is quite useful for multiple popular downstream tasks on review data. Thanks to an effective LLM-based predictor, our large-scale experiments and analyses can assist future endeavours to maximally benefit from the many available product reviews on the web. The revealed Types can also be used as-is for filtering information according to particular needs of customers or vendors, such as only showing descriptions of the product, or only suggesting improvements.
We advocate pursuing similar broad-ranged research on other domains, elaborating on our initial review-focused typology, enabling informed approaches for even more downstream applications.

The typology we suggest is partly inspired by previous related studies, and a result of a systematic examination of many review sentences which facilitated the accumulation of the typology types. There are likely other possible types and combinations of types that would establish an alternate typology. However, our research here mainly focuses on the use of a broad typology for revealing insights in product reviews and for assisting downstream applications.

In addition, our experiments and analyses rely on a predictor that does not label sentences at full accuracy. While our evaluation of the predictor gives us good reason to place confidence in its outputs, the conclusions we draw are certainly based on some noisy results. We therefore reported on findings from the analyses that are more distinctly evident from the data. Finally, when comparing or classifying between two subsets of texts (e.g., helpful vs. unhelpful, or reviews vs. summaries), the noise is analogously enforced on both subsets, making them generally comparable.

Lastly, our topology can inspire typologies in other review domains (such as hotels or dining) or other domains entirely. While some types in our typology are likely relevant and can be reused for other domains, developing this further requires a more thorough investigation in future work.

The 8 categories from which the sentences were taken initially for deriving Types are “Fashion”, “Automotive”, “Books”, “CDs and Vinyls”, “Digital Music”, “Electronics”, “Movies and TV”, “Toys and Games”. These were chosen due to the expected differences in aspects associated to the categories, to allow variability and ensure an exhaustive view at types found within reviews.

The inappropriate Type was not found in the initial data we used, likely because e-commerce websites make an effort to remove such reviews. However this Type is a know characteristic in reviews data, and sometimes seeps in anyways.

We used NLTK (Bird, 2006) sentence tokenization to split reviews to sentences, and applied simple normalization techniques to clean the texts. This procedure was used also for the rest of the experiments and analyses in the paper.

All our initial assessments on the models were done on the development set. We tried several temperatures and engineered some prompts for the models. We only evaluated models that all its responses started with a \sayyes or \sayno.

We extracted optimal thresholds for all Types as follows: For thresholds [0.1,1.0][0.1,1.0] incrementing by 0.10.1, and for each Type, we computed the F1F_{1} score against the annotations. The threshold with the maximum F1F_{1} score was chosen as the optimal threshold for the corresponding Type.
The macro-F1F_{1} score for a model is then the average of F1F_{1} scores over all Types with their optimal thresholds. The main results on the development set are presented in Table 5.
Using 30 repetitions per prompt only slightly improves results, which is not worth the increased compute resources.

A supplementary manual inspection of the predictions gave reason to rely confidently enough on the flan-t5-xxl model. In fact, we noticed that many of the Types predicted by the models, that were not labeled in the development set, were indeed valid in certain contexts. In any case, our aim is not to produce the best model, but to rely on one enough for our analyses.

We note that all other models we tried gave substantially worse results, or irrelevant responses altogether. On the other hand, the models presented in Table 5 were very consistent both in output format and final results.

We set the thresholds for all Types with those computed on the development set. The F1F_{1} score was then computed for each Type on the test set, and the average of those produces the final macro-F1F_{1} score, as presented in Table 13. The recall and precision indicate a finer-grained view of the capabilities of the predictor. On manual inspection, many of the incorrectly labeled sentences could be correct in certain contexts. I.e., when the predictor labels incorrectly, it is not nonsensically wrong.

All FLAN models were run using the Huggingface transformers library (Wolf et al., 2020). The Jurassic model was run using the AI21 Python SDK (AI21Studio, 2023) with a very small monetary budget. In addition to flan-t5-{xl, xxl}, flan-ul2 and j2-jumbo-instruct, we also tested flan-t5-{base, large}, which produced considerably poorer outputs.

On the models assessed, we mainly tested temperatures of 0.30.3 and 0.70.7. The FLAN models worked better with 0.30.3 and the Jurassic model worked better with 0.70.7 (as evaluated on the development set). However, as said, only the flan-t5-xxl and flan-ul2 produced reasonable enough results in any case.
We ran all FLAN predictions on an AWS g4dn.12xlarge EC2 server, which includes 4 NVIDIA T4 GPUs, 64 GB GPU memory and 192 GB RAM. The Jurassic model runs through an API.

The flan-t5-xxl model’s run time was about 0.450.45 seconds per prompt (including the 10 repetitions for computing probability). Hence, each sentence required an average of 10.810.8 seconds for full typology prediction.
The chosen prompt used in the flan-t5-xxl model is:

The product category subprompt is a string prepared for each category. For example the subprompt for the ‘Electronics’ category would be “an electronics product”, and for ‘Toys and Games’ it would be “a toy or game”.
The Type question prompt substring is a question prepared for each Type, as found in Table 11.
An example of a prompt for a sentence from the “Books” category for the product usage Type would be:
The Type predictions on which we rely throughout this paper can be reproduced using these prompts with the flan-t5-xxl Huggingface model. Complementing code will be available as well.
This appendix describes the data, training, testing and evaluation details in all the experiments throughout the paper.

Gamzu et al. (2021) released a dataset of helpful sentences, linked to their full review data. The data is based on 123 products from categories “Toys”, “Books”, “Movies”, “Music”, “Camera” and “Electronics”. We targeted an approximately balanced amount of helpful to unhelpful reviews (regardless of the sentence-level helpfulness which Gamzu et al. target). Each review has ‘helpful-count’ and ‘nothelpful-count’ fields. By setting helpful-count >=9>=9 and nothelpful-count ==0==0 for helpful reviews, and nothelpful-count >=3>=3 and helpful-count ==0==0 for unhelpful reviews, we produce 458 and 486 reviews respectively. The aim was to collect about 500 reviews for each class.
We use 50 iterations of cross validation, with a train set of 70% and test if 30%. The procedure is repeated for the different Type sets separately.
As common in binary classification tasks, we report the accuracy measure (Yadav and Vishwakarma, 2020).
Table 6 extends Table 2, including all Type sets defined in section 2.

The full data of Gamzu et al. (2021) consists of 20000 sentences in the train set and 2000 in the test set. Each sentence has a continuous helpfulness score between 0 and 2. In addition, we find the helpfulness scores marking the borders of the top and bottom tertiles in the train set (1.41.4 and 1.01.0), and mark the sentences as helpful or unhelpful respectively, or neutral for the mid-section. We use the same border scores to mark the sentences in the test set. Overall there are (7475, 7072, 5453) (unhelpful, helpful, neutral) sentences in the train, and (742, 562, 696) in the test. Notice that classes are not perfectly balanced in the train set since the scores on the borders (1.4 and 1.0) repeat in many sentences.
The Linear Regression is conducted on the full original data. The SVM classification is done on the sentences marked with the helpful and unhelpful classes only (neutral ignored).
We use the metrics reported in (Gamzu et al., 2021) for the regression task, and accuracy for the binary classification task.
Table 6 and Table 7 extend Table 2 and Table 3 respectively, including all Type sets defined in section 2.

As in §C.1, we use the subset of products from Gamzu et al. (2021) for convenience. In order to prevent any bias from the helpfulness signal, we randomly sampled 5000 reviews (out of 58205) from the data without any feature pertaining to up-votes and down-votes. The review rating distribution is: {5:3337,4:675,3:321,2:219,1:448}\{\texttt{5}:3337,\texttt{4}:675,\texttt{3}:321,\texttt{2}:219,\texttt{1}:448\}, and the polarity hence distributes to: {positive:4012,negative:988}\{\texttt{positive}:4012,\texttt{negative}:988\}. There are an average of 4.054.05 sentences per review.

As in §C.1, we use 50 iterations of cross validation, with a train set of 70% and test of 30%, averaging results over the 50 iterations. The procedure is repeated for the different Type sets separately.
As in §C.1, we report the accuracy measure.
Table 6 extends Table 2, including all Type sets defined in section 2.

We iterated over the 31K products in the AmaSum dataset (Bražinskas et al., 2021), and filtered out the products without any product category assigned to it. Since products in this dataset are given several hierarchical category options, we heuristically assigned a category by manually clustering related category labels to some general ones. Then for 5 categories (“Books”, “Electronics”, “Apparel”, “Toys and Games”, “Pet Supplies”) with over 30 products, and chosen manually by their differing aspect-level characteristics, we randomly sampled 20 products. For these products we collected all their reviews and one reference summary (there are rare cases in AmaSum with more than one reference summary for a product). Overall there are 100 products with an average of 77.377.3 reviews per product (77297729 reviews total), 4.24.2 sentences per review, and 7.17.1 sentences per summary.
Like in other experiments, a review/summary level vector is the average of its sentence vectors. Review/summary level vectors are then averaged to get the final two vectors to compare (in Figure 3).

For the rhetorical structure analysis, the 6-sentence reviews behave similarly to reviews with other lengths (Figure 5). Less than 6 sentences does not emphasize the behavior visually as clearly. There are 763763 (out of 77297729) reviews with 6 sentences. For the analysis on summaries, the 7-sentence summaries had the highest number of instances (2424 out of 100100), and it is visually easier to see the patterns in the data due to the summaries containing 3 subsections (verdict, pros, cons), although other length summaries behave similarly. Here, the sentence vectors at each review/summary position are averaged in order to plot the graphs. In the figures, only the Types with observable changes that have probabilities above 0.2 throughout the review/summary are displayed.

In addition to the standard evaluation of our Types prediction model in Section 3.2, we additionally assessed our model’s performance on benchmarks of specific Types already identified in previous work, namely tip, opinion, and opinion with reason. The favorable results here only place further emphasis on the reliability of our model, which gives confidence to perform our analyses in Sections 4 and 5. This assessment is only supplemental to our evaluation in Section 3.2.

We obtain existing annotated datasets and establish training sets for the sole purpose of setting a prediction threshold for one of our Types, which is then used as the predicted label for the annotated task. We experiment with tuning over the entire training set as well as with much smaller subsets of 100 sentences.

Product tips are generally defined as short, concise, practical, self-contained pieces of advice on a product (Hirsch et al., 2021), for example: \sayRead the card that came with it and see all the red flags!. While previous work (Hirsch et al., 2021; Farber et al., 2022) characterized tips into finer-grained sub-types, our definition assumes a generic definition.

We use the data annotated by Hirsch et al. (2021) over Amazon reviews for non-tips or tips. The data used by Hirsch et al. (2021) for their experiments (3059 tips and 48,870 non-tip sentences) is slightly different from the data we used (3848 tips and 81,323 non-tips), since we do not apply the initial rule-based filtering that they enforce.

We noticed that some sentences were annotated in the dataset as tips, even though we do not view them as such, e.g., \sayThe ring that holds the card together is kind of flimsy. Since this was especially true under the warning sub-type, we removed these sentences from the data, to better represent our notion of a tip.

Our main goal is to show that our predictor performs decently, and not that we provide a better solution than Hirsch et al. (2021). Hence, exacting the data distribution is not a major concern. Of the available data, we use all 3,848 tip-sentences and sample twice as many non-tip sentences (out of the 81,323).
The dataset includes reviews from 5 categories: “Musical Instruments”, “Baby”, “Toys and Games”, “Tools and Home Improvement”, and “Sports and Outdoors”.

We follow the cross-validation (Xu and Liang, 2001) procedure of Hirsch et al. (2021) to train (find the best tip-Type threshold) and to respectively evaluate tip identification using the found threshold. For 50 iterations, all tips are used (or warning sub-types are removed) and the same amount of non-tips are sampled (out of the many available). Then the data is split to 80%/20% train/test splits. In case a train set size is forced, e.g., only 100 samples, then it is sampled from the training data. The train set is used to find the optimal threshold using Youden’s J statistic on the ROC curve (Youden, 1950). Then the test set is used to compute the different evaluation metrics. The results are averaged over the 50 iterations and reported, and the bootstrapping method (Efron, 1992) is used to compute confidence intervals at alpha=0.025 (95% confidence percentile). Since the results in Hirsch et al. (2021) do not report confidence intervals, we cannot fully compare to their results, however the differences are large enough to assume significant differences, as viewed in our findings.

Hirsch et al. (2021) report Recall@Precision scores, i.e., setting a specific precision value and producing the corresponding recall value. This approach is used since there is a tradeoff between presenting tips for more products and being confident about the tips presented. We also present the F1F_{1} of the tip/no-tip prediction, as common for classification tasks.

Product reviews are rich in opinions and are often more convincing when a reason is provided for the opinion. A persuasive saying, whether opinionated or objective, is a type of argument (Eckle-Kohler et al., 2015), a class found to be helpful for predicting review helpfulness (Liu et al., 2017; Passon et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2022). We thus turn to an argument mining dataset as an assessment benchmark.

The A​M2AM^{2} (Chen et al., 2022) dataset is annotated at the clause level for various sub-types of subjective and objective arguments and reasons. The data contains 878 reviews (pre-split to train and test sets) from 693 products in the “Headphones” category, and only sentences that are argumentative are kept in each review. Each review is broken down to its clauses (a sentence or smaller). A clause is annotated as “Policy”, “Value”, “Fact” or “Testimony”, where the first two are subjective and the latter two are objective. In addition, a clause can be marked as “Reason” or “Evidence”, where the former provides support for a subjective clause, and the latter for an objective clause. The support clauses are linked to relevant clauses that they support.

We automatically parse and re-label this data and create full-sentence-level instances for opinion and opinion with reason Types. If a sentence contains a subjective clause, it is marked as an opinion, and if it also contains a support clause then it is also marked as an opinion with reason. Otherwise a sentence is neither.
We end up with 3132 (2133 train, 999 test) opinion sentences, of which 363 (249 train, 114 test) are also opinion with reason, and 1359 (972 train, 387 test) non-opinion sentences.

We separately classify the opinion Type and the opinion with reason Types against the non-opinionated class. As in the case of tips, we use the train set to find the best threshold for the relevant Type, and then evaluate on the test set. We also experimented with sampling just 100 train instances for tuning the threshold.
As common in classification tasks, we report the F1F_{1} measure.

Table 9 presents the classification results on the three specific Type evaluations. Our zero-shot classifier, which only uses the training sets to find an optimal threshold for the corresponding Type, is able to identify tips and opinions effectively, and opinion with reason fairly well, on their respective benchmarks.888Chen et al. (2022) do not provide any intrinsic baseline results on opinion classification to which we can compare. In addition, we manipulated the original data from clause-level to sentence-level. Moreover, limiting the training sets to only 100 samples appears to hardly have any effect on the quality of the model, showing its robustness to paucity of labeled data.

For the tip task, we see in Table 10 that the predictor performs on par or much better than existing supervised baselines from Hirsch et al. (2021). Although our definition of tip differs slightly from that used in annotating the benchmark, our predictor is still reliable and useful. When the warning subtype of a tip is removed from the data (which, as mentioned before, generally do not fit our notion of a tip), the results dramatically improve.

subsections.title: Data.
paragraphs: The AmaSum product reviews summarization dataset (Bražinskas et al., 2021) consists of about 31K products, each associated with a list of reviews and a reference summary taken from online platforms for professional product reviews. Each summary comprises verdict, pros and cons sections. We perform some filtering heuristics and then sample 100 products with their reviews and summaries, representing five different product categories (see App. C.4), for a total of 7,729 reviews and 100 summaries.

subsections.title: Reviews vs. Summaries
paragraphs: We compare Type prominence between reviews and summaries by aggregating their probability vectors (averaging review- or summary-level Type vectors), presented in Figure 3. Comparing the Type probabilities in reviews and summaries, it is clear (and unsurprising) that summaries are considerably more efficient in communicating information. In particular, summaries predominantly describe the product, provide reasoning for opinions and offer many tips. These findings are consistent with Bražinskas et al. (2021), who describe the verdict as emphasizing the most important points about the product, and the pros and cons as providing details about different aspects of the product.

Reviewers seem to add a non-negligible amount of personal information to the parts eventually excluded from summaries, possibly to make their reviews more relatable, for example, \sayWe had used this book when my children were young and were identifying flowers. Interestingly, according to our analysis of helpfulness (Figure 2b), readers seem to find this characteristic to be less helpful. In general, we find (Table 4) a much higher correlation between the vector of summaries and that of helpful sentences, than between the vector of summaries and that of unhelpful sentences. In other words, good summaries tend to contain mainly helpful sentences. On the other hand, a helpful review has a lower correlation with summaries, meaning that it still contains some potential verbosity, which is natural given the less stringent length constraints.

subsections.title: Product Categories
paragraphs: We conduct an examination of the reviews at the product category level to learn what kind of information reviewers tend to share for different kinds of products. Figure 4 reveals, for example, that people share much more personal information when reviewing toys and games. We find that reviewers purchase these mostly for others, especially children, and therefore provide an explanation of who they bought the product for and their experience with it, e.g., \sayHopefully my 3 year old doesn’t toss all of the pieces everywhere. A review is also, expectedly, more likely to discuss setup of an electronics device than for other kinds of products. A book review is more likely to describe a situation or provide a tip, two Types we find often co-occurring within sentences suggesting usage for the book, e.g., \sayThis book should probably be combined with other study methods to help your child perform to the best of their ability. We suggest that this behaviour may be specific to certain subcategories of books, such as self-help and other nonfiction. Overall, analyzing variability of Types across product categories allows data-driven discovery of insights about products and customers.

subsections.title: Rhetorical Structure
paragraphs: Rhetorical Structure Theory studies the organization of a body of text and the relationship between its parts (Mann and Thompson, 1988), facilitating the assessment of a text’s coherence. In our setting, we can examine how Types affect each other as a review or summary progresses. To demonstrate this, we compute the average Type probabilities at each sentence position of reviews with 6 sentences, and summaries with 7 sentences (explained in Appendix C.4), and plot them on a graph showing the progression.

As can be seen in Figure 5, reviews tend to open and close with a buy decision statement. Customers use such statements to introduce the situation, e.g., \sayPurchased this SSD in order to put Windows 7 and some games on it to decrease load times., or conclude with an emphasizing reiteration of satisfaction (or lack thereof), e.g., \sayI only regret not getting it sooner! Conversely, reasoned opinions and detailed information tend to occur in the middle of the review. We note that the likelihood of speculative statements increases as the review progresses. Speculation should be delivered only after it is justified, e.g., \sayThat being said, they’re probably still ok for temporary storage.

AmaSum summaries are formed by concatenating a verdict (avg. 1.42 sentences in our data), a pros section (avg. 4.04 sentences) and a cons section (avg. 1.54 sentences). Due to this segmented configuration, the rhetorical structure is only partly relevant since global coherence is not an objective of the summary. Figure 6 demonstrates the behavior of the verdict at the first 1–2 sentences, the next ∼\sim4 sentences representing the structure of the pros, and the last 1–2 corresponding to the cons section. The verdict embodies features that stand out most in the product with its overall sentiment, e.g., \saySingle camera setup that’s reasonably priced because it doesn’t require a separate base station. Indeed, the product description and opinion with reason Types are particularly likely. The pros section generally describes advantages of the product ordered by importance, and accordingly we see reasoning and tip content peaking and then decreasing gradually along these sentences. The cons section provides increased speculation to suggest what the disadvantage might cause, e.g., \sayThe 16-hour battery life can become frustrating for some, as well as vastly increased incidence of improvement desire, such as \sayCustomers commented that these aren’t absorbent enough to handle big messes.
Future work may further examine review and summary structure through the lens of our typology to improve automatic review summarization and to control content type while maintaining coherence.

Product reviews contain a wealth of information that has been mined only shallowly over the years. We introduce a broad typology of information types that we show is quite useful for multiple popular downstream tasks on review data. Thanks to an effective LLM-based predictor, our large-scale experiments and analyses can assist future endeavours to maximally benefit from the many available product reviews on the web. The revealed Types can also be used as-is for filtering information according to particular needs of customers or vendors, such as only showing descriptions of the product, or only suggesting improvements.
We advocate pursuing similar broad-ranged research on other domains, elaborating on our initial review-focused typology, enabling informed approaches for even more downstream applications.

The typology we suggest is partly inspired by previous related studies, and a result of a systematic examination of many review sentences which facilitated the accumulation of the typology types. There are likely other possible types and combinations of types that would establish an alternate typology. However, our research here mainly focuses on the use of a broad typology for revealing insights in product reviews and for assisting downstream applications.

In addition, our experiments and analyses rely on a predictor that does not label sentences at full accuracy. While our evaluation of the predictor gives us good reason to place confidence in its outputs, the conclusions we draw are certainly based on some noisy results. We therefore reported on findings from the analyses that are more distinctly evident from the data. Finally, when comparing or classifying between two subsets of texts (e.g., helpful vs. unhelpful, or reviews vs. summaries), the noise is analogously enforced on both subsets, making them generally comparable.

Lastly, our topology can inspire typologies in other review domains (such as hotels or dining) or other domains entirely. While some types in our typology are likely relevant and can be reused for other domains, developing this further requires a more thorough investigation in future work.

The 8 categories from which the sentences were taken initially for deriving Types are “Fashion”, “Automotive”, “Books”, “CDs and Vinyls”, “Digital Music”, “Electronics”, “Movies and TV”, “Toys and Games”. These were chosen due to the expected differences in aspects associated to the categories, to allow variability and ensure an exhaustive view at types found within reviews.

The inappropriate Type was not found in the initial data we used, likely because e-commerce websites make an effort to remove such reviews. However this Type is a know characteristic in reviews data, and sometimes seeps in anyways.

We used NLTK (Bird, 2006) sentence tokenization to split reviews to sentences, and applied simple normalization techniques to clean the texts. This procedure was used also for the rest of the experiments and analyses in the paper.

All our initial assessments on the models were done on the development set. We tried several temperatures and engineered some prompts for the models. We only evaluated models that all its responses started with a \sayyes or \sayno.

We extracted optimal thresholds for all Types as follows: For thresholds [0.1,1.0][0.1,1.0] incrementing by 0.10.1, and for each Type, we computed the F1F_{1} score against the annotations. The threshold with the maximum F1F_{1} score was chosen as the optimal threshold for the corresponding Type.
The macro-F1F_{1} score for a model is then the average of F1F_{1} scores over all Types with their optimal thresholds. The main results on the development set are presented in Table 5.
Using 30 repetitions per prompt only slightly improves results, which is not worth the increased compute resources.

A supplementary manual inspection of the predictions gave reason to rely confidently enough on the flan-t5-xxl model. In fact, we noticed that many of the Types predicted by the models, that were not labeled in the development set, were indeed valid in certain contexts. In any case, our aim is not to produce the best model, but to rely on one enough for our analyses.

We note that all other models we tried gave substantially worse results, or irrelevant responses altogether. On the other hand, the models presented in Table 5 were very consistent both in output format and final results.

We set the thresholds for all Types with those computed on the development set. The F1F_{1} score was then computed for each Type on the test set, and the average of those produces the final macro-F1F_{1} score, as presented in Table 13. The recall and precision indicate a finer-grained view of the capabilities of the predictor. On manual inspection, many of the incorrectly labeled sentences could be correct in certain contexts. I.e., when the predictor labels incorrectly, it is not nonsensically wrong.

All FLAN models were run using the Huggingface transformers library (Wolf et al., 2020). The Jurassic model was run using the AI21 Python SDK (AI21Studio, 2023) with a very small monetary budget. In addition to flan-t5-{xl, xxl}, flan-ul2 and j2-jumbo-instruct, we also tested flan-t5-{base, large}, which produced considerably poorer outputs.

On the models assessed, we mainly tested temperatures of 0.30.3 and 0.70.7. The FLAN models worked better with 0.30.3 and the Jurassic model worked better with 0.70.7 (as evaluated on the development set). However, as said, only the flan-t5-xxl and flan-ul2 produced reasonable enough results in any case.
We ran all FLAN predictions on an AWS g4dn.12xlarge EC2 server, which includes 4 NVIDIA T4 GPUs, 64 GB GPU memory and 192 GB RAM. The Jurassic model runs through an API.

The flan-t5-xxl model’s run time was about 0.450.45 seconds per prompt (including the 10 repetitions for computing probability). Hence, each sentence required an average of 10.810.8 seconds for full typology prediction.
The chosen prompt used in the flan-t5-xxl model is:

The product category subprompt is a string prepared for each category. For example the subprompt for the ‘Electronics’ category would be “an electronics product”, and for ‘Toys and Games’ it would be “a toy or game”.
The Type question prompt substring is a question prepared for each Type, as found in Table 11.
An example of a prompt for a sentence from the “Books” category for the product usage Type would be:
The Type predictions on which we rely throughout this paper can be reproduced using these prompts with the flan-t5-xxl Huggingface model. Complementing code will be available as well.
This appendix describes the data, training, testing and evaluation details in all the experiments throughout the paper.

Gamzu et al. (2021) released a dataset of helpful sentences, linked to their full review data. The data is based on 123 products from categories “Toys”, “Books”, “Movies”, “Music”, “Camera” and “Electronics”. We targeted an approximately balanced amount of helpful to unhelpful reviews (regardless of the sentence-level helpfulness which Gamzu et al. target). Each review has ‘helpful-count’ and ‘nothelpful-count’ fields. By setting helpful-count >=9>=9 and nothelpful-count ==0==0 for helpful reviews, and nothelpful-count >=3>=3 and helpful-count ==0==0 for unhelpful reviews, we produce 458 and 486 reviews respectively. The aim was to collect about 500 reviews for each class.
We use 50 iterations of cross validation, with a train set of 70% and test if 30%. The procedure is repeated for the different Type sets separately.
As common in binary classification tasks, we report the accuracy measure (Yadav and Vishwakarma, 2020).
Table 6 extends Table 2, including all Type sets defined in section 2.

The full data of Gamzu et al. (2021) consists of 20000 sentences in the train set and 2000 in the test set. Each sentence has a continuous helpfulness score between 0 and 2. In addition, we find the helpfulness scores marking the borders of the top and bottom tertiles in the train set (1.41.4 and 1.01.0), and mark the sentences as helpful or unhelpful respectively, or neutral for the mid-section. We use the same border scores to mark the sentences in the test set. Overall there are (7475, 7072, 5453) (unhelpful, helpful, neutral) sentences in the train, and (742, 562, 696) in the test. Notice that classes are not perfectly balanced in the train set since the scores on the borders (1.4 and 1.0) repeat in many sentences.
The Linear Regression is conducted on the full original data. The SVM classification is done on the sentences marked with the helpful and unhelpful classes only (neutral ignored).
We use the metrics reported in (Gamzu et al., 2021) for the regression task, and accuracy for the binary classification task.
Table 6 and Table 7 extend Table 2 and Table 3 respectively, including all Type sets defined in section 2.

As in §C.1, we use the subset of products from Gamzu et al. (2021) for convenience. In order to prevent any bias from the helpfulness signal, we randomly sampled 5000 reviews (out of 58205) from the data without any feature pertaining to up-votes and down-votes. The review rating distribution is: {5:3337,4:675,3:321,2:219,1:448}\{\texttt{5}:3337,\texttt{4}:675,\texttt{3}:321,\texttt{2}:219,\texttt{1}:448\}, and the polarity hence distributes to: {positive:4012,negative:988}\{\texttt{positive}:4012,\texttt{negative}:988\}. There are an average of 4.054.05 sentences per review.

As in §C.1, we use 50 iterations of cross validation, with a train set of 70% and test of 30%, averaging results over the 50 iterations. The procedure is repeated for the different Type sets separately.
As in §C.1, we report the accuracy measure.
Table 6 extends Table 2, including all Type sets defined in section 2.

subsections.title: Development and Test Evaluation
paragraphs: All our initial assessments on the models were done on the development set. We tried several temperatures and engineered some prompts for the models. We only evaluated models that all its responses started with a \sayyes or \sayno.

We extracted optimal thresholds for all Types as follows: For thresholds [0.1,1.0][0.1,1.0] incrementing by 0.10.1, and for each Type, we computed the F1F_{1} score against the annotations. The threshold with the maximum F1F_{1} score was chosen as the optimal threshold for the corresponding Type.
The macro-F1F_{1} score for a model is then the average of F1F_{1} scores over all Types with their optimal thresholds. The main results on the development set are presented in Table 5.
Using 30 repetitions per prompt only slightly improves results, which is not worth the increased compute resources.

A supplementary manual inspection of the predictions gave reason to rely confidently enough on the flan-t5-xxl model. In fact, we noticed that many of the Types predicted by the models, that were not labeled in the development set, were indeed valid in certain contexts. In any case, our aim is not to produce the best model, but to rely on one enough for our analyses.

The recall and precision indicate a finer-grained view of the capabilities of the predictor. On manual inspection, many of the incorrectly labeled sentences could be correct in certain contexts. I.e., when the predictor labels incorrectly, it is not nonsensically wrong.

subsections.title: Development evaluation.
paragraphs: All our initial assessments on the models were done on the development set. We tried several temperatures and engineered some prompts for the models. We only evaluated models that all its responses started with a \sayyes or \sayno.

We extracted optimal thresholds for all Types as follows: For thresholds [0.1,1.0][0.1,1.0] incrementing by 0.10.1, and for each Type, we computed the F1F_{1} score against the annotations. The threshold with the maximum F1F_{1} score was chosen as the optimal threshold for the corresponding Type.
The macro-F1F_{1} score for a model is then the average of F1F_{1} scores over all Types with their optimal thresholds. The main results on the development set are presented in Table 5.
Using 30 repetitions per prompt only slightly improves results, which is not worth the increased compute resources.

A supplementary manual inspection of the predictions gave reason to rely confidently enough on the flan-t5-xxl model. In fact, we noticed that many of the Types predicted by the models, that were not labeled in the development set, were indeed valid in certain contexts. In any case, our aim is not to produce the best model, but to rely on one enough for our analyses.

We note that all other models we tried gave substantially worse results, or irrelevant responses altogether. On the other hand, the models presented in Table 5 were very consistent both in output format and final results.

subsections.title: Test evaluation.
paragraphs: We set the thresholds for all Types with those computed on the development set. The F1F_{1} score was then computed for each Type on the test set, and the average of those produces the final macro-F1F_{1} score, as presented in Table 13. The recall and precision indicate a finer-grained view of the capabilities of the predictor. On manual inspection, many of the incorrectly labeled sentences could be correct in certain contexts. I.e., when the predictor labels incorrectly, it is not nonsensically wrong.

subsections.title: Model
paragraphs: All FLAN models were run using the Huggingface transformers library (Wolf et al., 2020). The Jurassic model was run using the AI21 Python SDK (AI21Studio, 2023) with a very small monetary budget. In addition to flan-t5-{xl, xxl}, flan-ul2 and j2-jumbo-instruct, we also tested flan-t5-{base, large}, which produced considerably poorer outputs.

On the models assessed, we mainly tested temperatures of 0.30.3 and 0.70.7. The FLAN models worked better with 0.30.3 and the Jurassic model worked better with 0.70.7 (as evaluated on the development set). However, as said, only the flan-t5-xxl and flan-ul2 produced reasonable enough results in any case.
We ran all FLAN predictions on an AWS g4dn.12xlarge EC2 server, which includes 4 NVIDIA T4 GPUs, 64 GB GPU memory and 192 GB RAM. The Jurassic model runs through an API.

The flan-t5-xxl model’s run time was about 0.450.45 seconds per prompt (including the 10 repetitions for computing probability). Hence, each sentence required an average of 10.810.8 seconds for full typology prediction.
The chosen prompt used in the flan-t5-xxl model is:

The product category subprompt is a string prepared for each category. For example the subprompt for the ‘Electronics’ category would be “an electronics product”, and for ‘Toys and Games’ it would be “a toy or game”.
The Type question prompt substring is a question prepared for each Type, as found in Table 11.
An example of a prompt for a sentence from the “Books” category for the product usage Type would be:
The Type predictions on which we rely throughout this paper can be reproduced using these prompts with the flan-t5-xxl Huggingface model. Complementing code will be available as well.

subsections.title: Prediction of Review Helpfulness
paragraphs: Gamzu et al. (2021) released a dataset of helpful sentences, linked to their full review data. The data is based on 123 products from categories “Toys”, “Books”, “Movies”, “Music”, “Camera” and “Electronics”. We targeted an approximately balanced amount of helpful to unhelpful reviews (regardless of the sentence-level helpfulness which Gamzu et al. target). Each review has ‘helpful-count’ and ‘nothelpful-count’ fields. By setting helpful-count >=9>=9 and nothelpful-count ==0==0 for helpful reviews, and nothelpful-count >=3>=3 and helpful-count ==0==0 for unhelpful reviews, we produce 458 and 486 reviews respectively. The aim was to collect about 500 reviews for each class.
We use 50 iterations of cross validation, with a train set of 70% and test if 30%. The procedure is repeated for the different Type sets separately.
As common in binary classification tasks, we report the accuracy measure (Yadav and Vishwakarma, 2020).
Table 6 extends Table 2, including all Type sets defined in section 2.

subsections.title: Prediction of Review Sentence Helpfulness
paragraphs: The full data of Gamzu et al. (2021) consists of 20000 sentences in the train set and 2000 in the test set. Each sentence has a continuous helpfulness score between 0 and 2. In addition, we find the helpfulness scores marking the borders of the top and bottom tertiles in the train set (1.41.4 and 1.01.0), and mark the sentences as helpful or unhelpful respectively, or neutral for the mid-section. We use the same border scores to mark the sentences in the test set. Overall there are (7475, 7072, 5453) (unhelpful, helpful, neutral) sentences in the train, and (742, 562, 696) in the test. Notice that classes are not perfectly balanced in the train set since the scores on the borders (1.4 and 1.0) repeat in many sentences.
The Linear Regression is conducted on the full original data. The SVM classification is done on the sentences marked with the helpful and unhelpful classes only (neutral ignored).
We use the metrics reported in (Gamzu et al., 2021) for the regression task, and accuracy for the binary classification task.
Table 6 and Table 7 extend Table 2 and Table 3 respectively, including all Type sets defined in section 2.

subsections.title: Prediction of Sentiment Polarity
paragraphs: As in §C.1, we use the subset of products from Gamzu et al. (2021) for convenience. In order to prevent any bias from the helpfulness signal, we randomly sampled 5000 reviews (out of 58205) from the data without any feature pertaining to up-votes and down-votes. The review rating distribution is: {5:3337,4:675,3:321,2:219,1:448}\{\texttt{5}:3337,\texttt{4}:675,\texttt{3}:321,\texttt{2}:219,\texttt{1}:448\}, and the polarity hence distributes to: {positive:4012,negative:988}\{\texttt{positive}:4012,\texttt{negative}:988\}. There are an average of 4.054.05 sentences per review.

As in §C.1, we use 50 iterations of cross validation, with a train set of 70% and test of 30%, averaging results over the 50 iterations. The procedure is repeated for the different Type sets separately.
As in §C.1, we report the accuracy measure.
Table 6 extends Table 2, including all Type sets defined in section 2.

subsections.title: Analysis of Reviews and Summaries
paragraphs: We iterated over the 31K products in the AmaSum dataset (Bražinskas et al., 2021), and filtered out the products without any product category assigned to it. Since products in this dataset are given several hierarchical category options, we heuristically assigned a category by manually clustering related category labels to some general ones. Then for 5 categories (“Books”, “Electronics”, “Apparel”, “Toys and Games”, “Pet Supplies”) with over 30 products, and chosen manually by their differing aspect-level characteristics, we randomly sampled 20 products. For these products we collected all their reviews and one reference summary (there are rare cases in AmaSum with more than one reference summary for a product). Overall there are 100 products with an average of 77.377.3 reviews per product (77297729 reviews total), 4.24.2 sentences per review, and 7.17.1 sentences per summary.
Like in other experiments, a review/summary level vector is the average of its sentence vectors. Review/summary level vectors are then averaged to get the final two vectors to compare (in Figure 3).

For the rhetorical structure analysis, the 6-sentence reviews behave similarly to reviews with other lengths (Figure 5). Less than 6 sentences does not emphasize the behavior visually as clearly. There are 763763 (out of 77297729) reviews with 6 sentences. For the analysis on summaries, the 7-sentence summaries had the highest number of instances (2424 out of 100100), and it is visually easier to see the patterns in the data due to the summaries containing 3 subsections (verdict, pros, cons), although other length summaries behave similarly. Here, the sentence vectors at each review/summary position are averaged in order to plot the graphs. In the figures, only the Types with observable changes that have probabilities above 0.2 throughout the review/summary are displayed.

subsections.title: Tip Classification Evaluation
paragraphs: Product tips are generally defined as short, concise, practical, self-contained pieces of advice on a product (Hirsch et al., 2021), for example: \sayRead the card that came with it and see all the red flags!. While previous work (Hirsch et al., 2021; Farber et al., 2022) characterized tips into finer-grained sub-types, our definition assumes a generic definition.

We use the data annotated by Hirsch et al. (2021) over Amazon reviews for non-tips or tips. The data used by Hirsch et al. (2021) for their experiments (3059 tips and 48,870 non-tip sentences) is slightly different from the data we used (3848 tips and 81,323 non-tips), since we do not apply the initial rule-based filtering that they enforce.

We noticed that some sentences were annotated in the dataset as tips, even though we do not view them as such, e.g., \sayThe ring that holds the card together is kind of flimsy. Since this was especially true under the warning sub-type, we removed these sentences from the data, to better represent our notion of a tip.

Our main goal is to show that our predictor performs decently, and not that we provide a better solution than Hirsch et al. (2021). Hence, exacting the data distribution is not a major concern. Of the available data, we use all 3,848 tip-sentences and sample twice as many non-tip sentences (out of the 81,323).
The dataset includes reviews from 5 categories: “Musical Instruments”, “Baby”, “Toys and Games”, “Tools and Home Improvement”, and “Sports and Outdoors”.

We follow the cross-validation (Xu and Liang, 2001) procedure of Hirsch et al. (2021) to train (find the best tip-Type threshold) and to respectively evaluate tip identification using the found threshold. For 50 iterations, all tips are used (or warning sub-types are removed) and the same amount of non-tips are sampled (out of the many available). Then the data is split to 80%/20% train/test splits. In case a train set size is forced, e.g., only 100 samples, then it is sampled from the training data. The train set is used to find the optimal threshold using Youden’s J statistic on the ROC curve (Youden, 1950). Then the test set is used to compute the different evaluation metrics. The results are averaged over the 50 iterations and reported, and the bootstrapping method (Efron, 1992) is used to compute confidence intervals at alpha=0.025 (95% confidence percentile). Since the results in Hirsch et al. (2021) do not report confidence intervals, we cannot fully compare to their results, however the differences are large enough to assume significant differences, as viewed in our findings.

Hirsch et al. (2021) report Recall@Precision scores, i.e., setting a specific precision value and producing the corresponding recall value. This approach is used since there is a tradeoff between presenting tips for more products and being confident about the tips presented. We also present the F1F_{1} of the tip/no-tip prediction, as common for classification tasks.

subsections.title: Opinions Classification Evaluation
paragraphs: Product reviews are rich in opinions and are often more convincing when a reason is provided for the opinion. A persuasive saying, whether opinionated or objective, is a type of argument (Eckle-Kohler et al., 2015), a class found to be helpful for predicting review helpfulness (Liu et al., 2017; Passon et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2022). We thus turn to an argument mining dataset as an assessment benchmark.

The A​M2AM^{2} (Chen et al., 2022) dataset is annotated at the clause level for various sub-types of subjective and objective arguments and reasons. The data contains 878 reviews (pre-split to train and test sets) from 693 products in the “Headphones” category, and only sentences that are argumentative are kept in each review. Each review is broken down to its clauses (a sentence or smaller). A clause is annotated as “Policy”, “Value”, “Fact” or “Testimony”, where the first two are subjective and the latter two are objective. In addition, a clause can be marked as “Reason” or “Evidence”, where the former provides support for a subjective clause, and the latter for an objective clause. The support clauses are linked to relevant clauses that they support.

We automatically parse and re-label this data and create full-sentence-level instances for opinion and opinion with reason Types. If a sentence contains a subjective clause, it is marked as an opinion, and if it also contains a support clause then it is also marked as an opinion with reason. Otherwise a sentence is neither.
We end up with 3132 (2133 train, 999 test) opinion sentences, of which 363 (249 train, 114 test) are also opinion with reason, and 1359 (972 train, 387 test) non-opinion sentences.

We separately classify the opinion Type and the opinion with reason Types against the non-opinionated class. As in the case of tips, we use the train set to find the best threshold for the relevant Type, and then evaluate on the test set. We also experimented with sampling just 100 train instances for tuning the threshold.
As common in classification tasks, we report the F1F_{1} measure.
